title: Retry Backoff Tuner
summary: Push retry timing until it either helps recovery or makes the outage your fault.
question: When does a "helpful" retry loop turn into self-inflicted load?
result: Capped exponential backoff with jitter stopped recovery from turning into a dogpile.
appliedTo: Provider recovery path for scores, rankings, and scheduled news pulls.
signals: Burst risk climbs fast when retries stack before provider health recovers.
Base delay mattered more than max retry count once outages crossed the first minute.
Jitter kept concurrent workers from syncing themselves into request storms.

title: Cache TTL Ladder
summary: Tune cache windows until the feed feels fresh without bullying the API.
question: What can stay cached, and what actually has to feel live?
result: A per-surface TTL ladder kept scores fresh and stopped slower data from wasting budget.
appliedTo: Read paths for live scores, rankings, schedules, and generated scoreboards.
signals: Short TTLs were fine for scores but wasted budget on slower-moving standings data.
Prewarming mattered most during burst windows rather than normal traffic.
The right answer was not one TTL, but a ladder per content type.

title: Scheduler Heartbeat View
summary: Make silent scheduler drift obvious before users feel it.
question: How do you spot jobs that are technically up but already lying to you?
result: Heartbeat age plus stale thresholds made invisible failures visible enough to fix.
appliedTo: Scheduler health surface for background posting and queue-driven automations.
signals: A stale heartbeat reads more clearly than raw uptime when jobs silently stop doing useful work.
Health thresholds need context; one global threshold created noisy false alarms.
Auto-heal only helped once the stale state was obvious enough to trust.

title: Queue Burst Sim
summary: Shove game-day load through the queue and watch when users start feeling the lag.
question: How far behind can the worker pool get before the product feels sloppy?
result: Smoother dispatch and better priority routing hid burstiness better than just adding workers.
appliedTo: Worker allocation, priority routing, and queue smoothing during peak traffic windows.
signals: Backlog shape mattered more than raw queue size when judging the user-visible impact.
Priority routing helped when the queue was only slightly behind, not when it was already overwhelmed.
Worker count alone was less useful than smoother dispatch behavior.

title: Event Fingerprint Audit
summary: Replay messy provider events until dedupe stops being theoretical.
question: How do you kill duplicates without throwing away the weird real updates?
result: Hybrid fingerprints plus fallback keys cut spam without losing edge cases.
appliedTo: Event normalization and replay handling for live update ingestion.
signals: Strict hashes looked clean until providers changed field order or omitted secondary IDs.
Fallback keys rescued edge cases but quickly raised duplicate risk if left too broad.
Replay testing found the dangerous failures faster than production logs did.

title: Dashboard Auth Flow
summary: Harden the admin path until it still feels usable at 2 a.m.
question: How much security friction can the ops surface take before people work around it?
result: Role gates, explicit sessions, and token rotation made the risky paths safer without wrecking response speed.
appliedTo: Operator dashboard auth, privileged routes, and admin-only mutations.
signals: Short sessions improved safety but created friction during incident response.
Role gates mattered more than extra login friction once inside the dashboard.
Token rotation was only useful once the session path itself was made explicit.

title: Ops Depth Map
summary: A candid miss: the 3d incident view looked cool and explained problems slower.
question: Can a spatial ops view help, or is it mostly showing off?
result: Some contrast cues survived. The 3d framing did not.
appliedTo: Operator experiments around incident visibility and queue-state storytelling.
signals: Depth added novelty, but dense overlays increased read time under pressure.
Labeling made the experiment usable; without it, the visualization became decoration.
The experiment still paid off because its contrast cues carried over into flatter runtime views.
